feed.title: Combined summary - Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Combined summary - Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary: The project's focus on extending TinyGo to produce RISC-V ELF binaries that run on the risc0 platform marks a significant development in cryptographic proof generation. By enabling zk-STARK proofs for arbitrary programs, the initiative showcases a practical application by demonstrating the generation of proofs confirming the derivation of a Taproot output public key from a BIP-32 seed using a BIP-86 path. This capability is particularly crucial for ensuring security in a potential post-quantum cryptographic scenario where classical asymmetric cryptography may be vulnerable. The use of a modified version of TinyGo, tailored for RISC-V 32-bit support necessary for risc0, along with Go utilities packaged into tinygo-zkvm, facilitates this process. These tools are integral to processing the RISC-V ELF binary, executing it, and generating the required cryptographic proofs, with key components hosted on GitHub in repositories such as [tinygo-zkvm](https://github.com/Roasbeef/tinygo-zkvm), [risc0](https://github.com/Roasbeef/risc0), and [go-zkvm](https://github.com/Roasbeef/go-zkvm). In recent communications, there has been a proposal to simplify the cryptographic circuit used in generating these proofs by focusing on proving the knowledge of an extended private key (xpriv) rather than the derivation of a taproot output key from a BIP39 seed. This adjustment could potentially reduce the computational burden, minimizing the number of cryptographic operations required. The streamlined approach involves computing the xpriv outside the circuit and inputting it into the guest program, which then calculates and outputs the corresponding extended public key (xpub). Verification of this proof checks the derived xpub against additional key-path elements provided by the prover, confirming the derivation without needing complex internal circuit operations. Moreover, discussions have included potential enhancements to scaling efficiency, such as using a single STARK proof to authorize transactions from multiple UTXOs within the same account, significantly reducing the verification workload. Further elaborations on reducing circuit complexity were discussed, highlighting the removal of elliptic curve point arithmetic and focusing primarily on the HMAC-SHA512 operation. This method simplifies the process and potentially enhances prover runtime dramatically. Such optimizations aim to strike an optimal balance between circuit simplicity and security assurance, setting new standards for cryptographic procedures in constrained computational environments like blockchain technologies. Additionally, recent optimization efforts have successfully reduced the size of cryptographic proofs, employing recursion layer techniques and exploring various methods to streamline proof systems further. These include comparing streamlined methods against existing approaches to determine if they result in smaller final proof sizes and creating custom Arithmetic Intermediate Representations (AIR) to minimize overhead introduced by RISC-V emulation in the risc0 proof chain. These advancements reflect significant progress in optimizing blockchain technology's efficiency and scalability, particularly in areas requiring compact yet complex proofs. Further resources developed provide detailed documentation on these new proof variants and their results, essential for understanding the specifics of the cryptographic improvements made.
